TECHNICAL APPROACH — Sample (federal VA healthcare-facility construction MATOC). Real winning technical-volume language to model a Technical Approach on.

1. EXPERIENCE / WHY WE ARE A GOOD CANDIDATE
The team's principals, management, and employees have extensive experience in the healthcare environment through numerous projects at Department of Veterans Affairs Health and Medical Centers nationwide. The team is acutely aware that conditions affecting construction, maintenance, and repair in a healthcare environment vary building to building, floor to floor, and room to room — being cognizant of these micro and macro environments lets the team respond flexibly and immediately to client needs. Representative VA projects: clinic reception renovations, operating-room ceiling replacement, baffle-ceiling replacement, bullet-proof pass-through windows, sharps/glove-box installation, data-center renovation, grease-trap relocation, roof/screen-enclosure repair. The OR Ceiling Renovations project best demonstrates healthcare-environment awareness: demolition and installation of a new ceiling system and walls (remove acoustical panels and lighting, demo sink and pipe, install drywall, doors, lighting, outlets, sprinkler heads, HVAC duct, access panels), with painting, final clean-up, dust barriers, and restricted pedestrian traffic. Where pressurization, air quality, or patient comfort must be adhered to, the team has the experience and knowledge to deliver the required quality.

1B. SHORT-NOTICE REQUIREMENTS — prepared to respond within contract emergency response times; relationships with local subcontractors centrally located to the facilities give wide reach; national supplier accounts aid quick response.
1C. PERCENTAGE REQUIREMENTS — the team self-performs Construction Management functions (weekly project meetings, document control, quality control, safety, submittals, site supervision, subcontractor screening, security, scheduling) to exceed the 15% GC requirement, and self-performs specialty trades (layout, stud/drywall, ceilings, doors, windows/hardware, finishes, painting, some flooring/concrete) to exceed the 25% specialty requirement.

2. ORGANIZATION — org chart; named principals with levels of responsibility (Principal; Senior VP/Quality Control Manager; Executive VP/Project Manager; Project Executive/Safety Manager); list of in-house trade employees by trade (carpentry, painting, flooring, demolition, doors, ceilings); interface with the home office via national accounts (Hertz, United Rental, Grainger, Graybar, HD Supply).

3. SPECIFIC PERSONNEL — resumes attached; list of major subcontractors (roofing, plumbing, HVAC, electrical) with prior joint VA/medical experience demonstrated.

4. SCHEDULING — develop a schedule using Microsoft Project or Primavera to define milestones, budgets, staffing, and benchmark deliverables; identify risk areas and risk levels; build a Work Breakdown Structure (WBS) with tasks/subtasks, skill/knowledge requirements; link tasks by precedence and show the Critical Path.

5. QUALITY CONTROL — two key elements: (a) a staff function with project-wide visibility dedicated to QC, including a Quality Control Representative (QCR) program that involves first-line supervisors, formulated from an ISO 9001 program; (b) a QC plan defining organizational responsibilities, inspection schedules/frequencies, surveillance plan, reporting, corrective-action process, and documentation. The QC Officer resides at the field office, reports to the TO Project Manager, and is unencumbered by operational duties. Initial QC planning produces a draft Quality Control Plan defining inspection/documentation/reporting to satisfy the TORFP; special controls and inspection systems keyed to VA design criteria; checklists covering procedural compliance, design documentation, safety compliance, PPE, housekeeping, tool/equipment condition, property accountability, and security. Corrective actions: track construction deficiencies from identification through corrective action to verification; daily inspections; examine materials/equipment against approved submittals; hazard analysis; punch lists documented in the daily QC Inspection Report with estimated correction times and follow-up inspection. Final task-order-specific QC Plan submitted after COR review.

6. SAFETY — effective safety requires management commitment to provide environment, encouragement, and support. Project-specific safety plans (tailored per project, submitted per OSHA requirements and the Installation Design Plan) include: new job start-up and weekly safety program; first-aid supplies and locations; required PPE; vehicle inspection checklist; fire prevention/control; posting of emergency numbers, insurance info, hospital-route maps, and required posters; weekly safety meetings including subcontractors; chain of command; hazard-analysis sheets. Promote safety via supervisor safety meetings, toolbox meetings, mandatory hard hats/reflective vests in construction areas, signs/posters, preventive-maintenance inspections, and motor-vehicle safety rules.

7. INFECTION CONTROL — adhere to construction specifications for infection control: isolate the HVAC system in the work area to prevent duct contamination; complete critical barriers / control-cube method before construction; maintain negative air pressure with HEPA-equipped air filtration; construct anterooms and HEPA-vacuum personnel; require shoe covers; do not remove barriers until cleared by Environmental Services; HEPA-vacuum and wet-mop with disinfectant; contain construction waste in tightly covered containers. High-risk areas requiring special attention: emergency services, newborn nurseries, all operating rooms and sterile processing, cardiac catheterization/angiography, pharmacy admixture, oncology. The team is knowledgeable in Construction Activity Types A–D and Infection Control Risk Groups 1–4 (green/yellow/orange/red), and uses HEPA-filter vacuums, air scrubbers (Type A HEPA captures 99.97% of 0.3-micron particles), sticky mats, plastic containment partitions, shoe booties, and Tyvek suits to meet ICRA requirements.
